elements.sent-2411-001: Recent efforts in Gaussian-Splat-based Novel View Synthesis can achieve photorealistic rendering; however, such capability is limited in sparse-view scenarios due to sparse initialization and over-fitting floaters.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent progress in depth estimation and alignment can provide dense point cloud with few views; however, the resulting pose accuracy is suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we present SPARS3R, which combines the advantages of accurate pose estimation from Structure-from-Motion and dense point cloud from depth estimation.
elements.sent-2411-004: To this end, SPARS3R first performs a Global Fusion Alignment process that maps a prior dense point cloud to a sparse point cloud from Structure-from-Motion based on triangulated correspondences.
elements.sent-2411-005: RANSAC is applied during this process to distinguish inliers and outliers.
elements.sent-2411-006: SPARS3R then performs a second, Semantic Outlier Alignment step, which extracts semantically coherent regions around the outliers and performs local alignment in these regions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Along with several improvements in the evaluation process, we demonstrate that SPARS3R can achieve photorealistic rendering with sparse images and significantly outperforms existing approaches.. \footnote{Code: \href{https://github.com/snldmt/SPARS3R {https://github.com/snldmt/SPARS3R.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Photorealistic scene reconstruction and Novel View Synthesis (NVS) from unposed 2D images is a challenging task with numerous applications in site modeling, autonomous driving, robotics, urban and agricultural planning, etc. The introduction of methods such as Neural Radiance Field (NeRF)~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl20233d have introduced significant advances in rendering quality and efficiency based on dense multi-view imagery.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, applications of these methods still face issues in practical scenarios where a dense coverage of the scene is not available.. \captionsetup[subfigure]{labelformat=empty.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{@{}c@{}c@{}} \begin{subfigure}{.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=0.66\textwidth]{figures/teaser/Instant-ngp-min.png} \caption{ Instant-NGP~\cite{muller2022instant}} \label{fs} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=0.66\textwidth]{figures/teaser/fsgs-min.png} \caption{ FSGS~\cite{zhu2025fsgs}} \label{fg} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=0.66\textwidth]{figures/teaser/instantsplat-min.png} \caption{ InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat}} \label{ps} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=0.66\textwidth]{figures/teaser/ours-min.png} \caption{ SPARS3R} \label{pg} \end{subfigure} \end{tabular} \caption{A visualization of SPAS3R in comparison to current SoTA. Without additional prior, sparse NVS leads to incorrect geometry by Instant-NGP~\cite{muller2022instant}. FSGS~\cite{zhu2025fsgs} can be blurry due to sparse initialization and insufficient densification. InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat} relies on DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r} initialization with suboptimal poses. Our method, SPARS3R, can reliably render details in the foreground and background with accurate poses. } \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-010: Given sparse images, over-fitting the photometric objectives to an incorrect geometry is a common issue in NVS.
elements.sent-2411-011: Various constraints, such as semantic consistent loss \cite{jain2021putting , depth and novel-view regularization \cite{niemeyer2022regnerf, deng2022depth, wang2023sparsenerf , frequency regularization \cite{yang2023freenerf , and ray entropy minimization \cite{kim2022infonerf have been introduced to improve upon NeRF.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These methods often lead to significant computation overhead due to the costly patch rendering from ray-tracing.
elements.sent-2411-013: More recently, Gaussian-Splatting-based approaches have further improved sparse NVS by leveraging the explicit representation and fast differentiable rasterization.
elements.sent-2411-014: Depth regularization \cite{li2024dngaussian , Gaussian floater pruning \cite{xiong2023sparsegs , and proximity-based Gaussian densification strategy~\cite{zhu2025fsgs have been proposed to constrain and guide the scene structures.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: As demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser , despite improved geometry, these approaches tend to produce overly smooth rendering in the background.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This issue can be attributed to a sparse initial point cloud, particularly in the background regions.
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, the additional constraints, e.g. based on monocular depth prior, are oftentimes imperfect, leading to noisy gradients that prevent proper densification in sparse regions.
elements.sent-2411-018: One potential solution to address this conundrum is to initialize Gaussian optimization with a denser point cloud to help disambiguate shape and radiance.
elements.sent-2411-019: To this end, recent advances in two-view depth estimation, particularly DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r and MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding , have shown the impressive ability to construct a dense point cloud from a pre-trained prior model.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In theory, such a point cloud can be directly used to optimize a Gaussian-Splat radiance field~\cite{fan2024instantsplat .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In practice, camera calibration obtained from multi-view depth alignment is often suboptimal due to the difficulties in estimating an accurate depth map.
elements.sent-2411-022: If left uncorrected, the Gaussian optimization process will generate floaters to compensate the suboptimal calibration, prompting strategies to prevent densification as a stopgap measure~\cite{fan2024instantsplat .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In comparison, camera calibration based on Structure-from-Motion, e.g., COLMAP~\cite{schoenberger2016colmap , is fast and accurate with an appropriate feature matching model.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Instead of aligning dense depth, SfM takes confident correspondences and performs triangulation with RANSAC~\cite{fischler1981random to reject outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In a sparse-view setting, such a process can be repeated numerous times to further improve accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address sparse point cloud initialization and pose inaccuracy in sparse-view NVS, we propose SPARS3R.
elements.sent-2411-027: SPARS3R composes of two stages: Global Fusion Alignment and Semantic Outlier Alignment.
elements.sent-2411-028: In Global Fusion Alignment, SPARS3R first obtains a dense point cloud from sparse views through DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r or MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding , and a sparse point cloud from COLMAP~\cite{schoenberger2016colmap .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: By taking the triangulated correspondences within images, SPARS3R fuses the dense point cloud onto the sparse point cloud through a global Procrustes Alignment process with RANSAC~\cite{fischler1981random .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Since some points will yield large error due to local scale variations, a second Semantic Alignment process is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, outliers from Global Fusion Alignment are identified and prompted through an Interactive Segmentation model, e.g. SAM~\cite{kirillov2023sam .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The resulting semantic masks indicate regions within dense point cloud to be treated with local alignments.
elements.sent-2411-033: After transforming these regions to the SfM point cloud, we obtain a dense and pose-wise accurate point cloud as a strong prior for Gaussian optimization.
elements.sent-2411-034: In summary, our contributions can be summarized as:.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a Gloabl Fusion Alignment approach, which transforms a prior dense point cloud onto a reference SfM sparse point cloud, putting dense initialization and accurate camera poses in the same coordinate frame.
elements.sent-2411-036: To address outliers that cannot be aligned accurately due to depth discrepancies, we propose a Semantic Outlier Alignment step.
elements.sent-2411-037: This step extracts semantically similar regions around the outliers to perform local alignment, resulting in a dense point cloud with minimum transformation error.
elements.sent-2411-038: We evaluate the overall method, SPARS3R, on three popular benchmark datasets and find significant quantitative and visual improvements compared to current SoTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-039: Photorealistic scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis is a long-standing task in computer vision and graphics.
elements.sent-2411-040: Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) \cite{mildenhall2021nerf proposes to model a scene implicitly with multi-layer perceptron (MLP) by mapping 3D coordinates and view direction to color and density, then rendering pixel value via alpha blending.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Since NeRF's introduction, numerous work has sought to improve its efficiency \cite{chen2022tensorf, fridovich2022plenoxels, garbin2021fastnerf, muller2022instant, reiser2021kilonerf, sun2022direct , quality \cite{barron2021mip, barron2022mip360, barron2023zip, chen2022aug, verbin2022ref , and extension to dynamic and in-the-wild \cite{pumarola2021dnerf,park2021nerfies,wu2022d2nerf,cao2023hexplane,shao2023tensor4d, martin2021nerfinthewild,fridovich2023kplanes,chen2022hanerf,yang2023crnerf,chen2024nerfhugs scenarios.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) \cite{kerbl20233d has emerged as an efficient alternative, offering improved rendering quality over NeRF.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Unlike NeRF, 3DGS represents scenes with explicit 3D Gaussians kernels and uses a differentiable rasterization technique.
elements.sent-2411-044: Following its introduction, many methods have emerged to reduce the computational cost \cite{lee2024compactgs,niedermayr2024compressed,navaneet2024compgs,girish2023eagles,fan2023lightgaussian and enhance quality \cite{yu2024mipsplat,yan2024multi,lu2024scaffold,cheng2024gaussianpro,niemeyer2024radsplat,zhang2024pixelgs of 3DGS.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: While 3DGS performs well with dense view support, its effectiveness decreases in practical settings with sparse inputs.
elements.sent-2411-046: Many approaches address this through depth constraints.
elements.sent-2411-047: FSGS \cite{zhu2025fsgs uses a Proximity-guided Gaussian Unpooling to mitigate the sparse initialization issue and introduces pseudo-views during training to avoid overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-048: DRGS~\cite{chung2024drgs and SparseGS~\cite{xiong2023sparsegs add a depth regularization term to enforce consistency between estimated and monocular depths while promoting smoothness.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: DNGS \cite{li2024dngs refines depth regularization by prioritizing local depth variations.}
elements.sent-2411-050: CoherentGS \cite{paliwal2025coherentgs initializes Gaussians from monocular depth estimation and improves coherence through an optical flow constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-051: InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat is a concurrent work that directly uses DUSt3R to produce dense point clouds and introduces a camera pose optimization strategy; densification of Gaussians is disabled to prevent the introduction of floaters due to suboptimal poses.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/pipeline_figure/pipeline.pdf} \caption{SPARS3R combines a prior dense point cloud $\chi$ and a sparse SfM point cloud $\widebar{\mathbf{X}}$. The prior $\chi$ often has inferior depth accuracy compared to $\widebar{\mathbf{X}}$. SPARS3R first globally aligns all points in $\chi$ onto $\widebar{\mathbf{X}}$, based on shared correspondences. \textcolor{green}{Inliers} and \textcolor{red}{outliers} are identified through alignment error. SPARS3R then extracts the semantically relevant 2D regions around the outliers to move local regions of $\chi$ in groups, producing a dense point cloud $\chi^*$ that is depth-wise and pose-wise accurate. By providing $\chi^*$ for Gaussian optimization, SPARS3R achieves photorealistic rendering under sparse-view condition.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-052: DUSt3R \cite{wang2024dust3r is a two-view depth estimation method that produces dense 3D point clouds from image pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Specifically, given an image pair $I^{1}$ and $I^{2}$, DUSt3R generates dense pointmaps $\mathbf{X ^{1,1}$ and $\mathbf{X ^{2,1 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W \times 3}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: A pointmap, \eg $\mathbf{X ^{1,1}$, provides a mapping from each pixel $(u,v)$ in image $I^1$ to a corresponding 3D point $\mathbf{X ^{1,1 (u,v)$ under the coordinate system of image $I^1$, giving dense 2D-to-3D correspondences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Given $K>2$ input images, DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r aggregates across all pairwise pointmap predictions by globally aligning pairwise pointmaps into a unified point cloud $\chi$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: This alignment process also produces camera parameters $\{W(n), J(n)\}$, which are the world-to-camera transformation matrix and the Jacobian of the affine approximation of the projective transformation for view $I^n$.
elements.sent-2411-057: As such, the relationship between a pixel $(u,v)$ on $I^n$ and its 3D point $\chi_i$ can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:dust3r_global_align} \begin{gathered} x_i = [u,v]^T = J(n)W(n) \chi_i \end{gathered}\end{equation} Building on this, MASt3R \cite{leroy2024grounding incorporates an additional matching head that generates discriminative local feature descriptors and pixel-level correspondences across paired images.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We note that while MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding uses this feature correspondence during its multi-depth alignment, the estimated poses are oftentimes inaccurate due to smooth depth predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-059: As show in \cref{fig:pipeline , SPARS3R builds upon the advancement in DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r and MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding as a pre-trained 3D prior for sparse-view reconstruction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Firstly, SPARS3R performs SfM based on image correspondences, either from MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding or other feature matching methods.}
elements.sent-2411-061: SPARS3R then aligns the dense point cloud produced by MASt3R via rigid transformations in two stages: Global Fusion Alignment and Semantic Outlier Alignment.
elements.sent-2411-062: While DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r and MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding can generate dense 3D points from sparse images, it does not estimate accurate or detailed depth through its pointmap formulation.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: As a result, the camera calibration obtained from such smooth depths is suboptimal and will degrade the fidelity of scene reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-064: To construct a better point cloud prior, we propose to align MASt3R's point cloud with that from a SfM pipeline, which is more reliable based on selective correspondences.
elements.sent-2411-065: This alignment process begins by identifying matching points between the SfM and MASt3R point clouds, denoted as $ \widebar{\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{\widebar{N}\times 3}$ and $\chi \in \mathbb{R ^{N\times 3}$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: A visibility indicator $V \in \mathbb{R ^{N\times 3 \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{\widetilde{N}\times 3}$ denotes if an SfM point $\widebar{\mathbf{X _i$ is visible with respect to a view $I^n$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: To simplify the process, we first find the image that has the highest number of visible SfM points: \begin{equation}n^* = \underset{n}{\arg \max } \ | V(\widebar{X},n)|, \ \ \widetilde{N} = |V(\widebar{X},n^{*})|\end{equation} Similar to \cref{Eq:dust3r_global_align , we can obtain the pixel-to-3D relationship of $(\widebar{u ,\widebar{v )$ and $V(\widebar{\mathbf{X ,n^{* )_i$ through estimated $\widebar{W}$ and $\widebar{J}$ from SfM.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The correspondence between a point $V(\widebar{\mathbf{X ,n^{* )_i$ and a point in $\chi$ can be established through a forward-backward projection: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:project_2D} \begin{gathered} \{ \widebar{x}_i \}_{i=1}^{\widetilde{N}}, \ \ \widebar{x}_i = [\widebar{u},\widebar{v}]^T = \widebar{J}({n^*}) \widebar{W}({n^*}) V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}},n^{*})_i, \widebar{\chi}_i = W^{-1}(n^*) J^{-1}(n^*) \widebar{x}_i \end{gathered}\end{equation} We denote the set of projected 2D SfM points as $\{ \widebar{x _i \}_{i=1 ^{\widetilde{N }$, and the set of 3D points that correspond to the same pixel in $\chi$ as $\widebar{\chi}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: With these identified correspondences, we can apply Procrustes Analysis~\cite{gower1975generalized to estimate a global rigid transformation, optimizing for scale, rotation, and translation.}
elements.sent-2411-070: During this process, RANSAC is applied to filter outliers, excluding their influence on the estimation.
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, we minimize the following objective to get the optimal global transformation based on Algorithm ~\ref{algo : \begin{equation}\label{Eq:procurstes_align} \begin{gathered} s_0, R_0, t_0 = \underset{s, R, t}{\arg \min } \sum_{i=1}^{\widetilde{N}} \left\|s R \widebar{\chi}_i +t - V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}}, n^*)_i \right\|^2 . \end{gathered}\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:procurstes_align} \begin{gathered} s_0, R_0, t_0 = \underset{s, R, t}{\arg \min } \sum_{i=1}^{\widetilde{N}} \left\|s R \widebar{\chi}_i +t - V(\widebar{X}, n^*)_i \right\|^2 . \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{RANSAC Alignment} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Matched MASt3R points $\widebar{\chi}$ and SfM points $V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}}, n^*)$, sample size $n$, error threshold $\epsilon$, number of iterations $R$ \STATE Output: Best parameters $(s_0, R_0, t_0)$, inlier set $\mathcal{I}$, outlier set $\mathcal{O}$, outlier SfM set $\mathcal{P}_{O}$ \STATE Initialize: $\text{num\_inliers} \leftarrow 0$, $(\hat{s_0}, \hat{R_0}, \hat{t_0}) \leftarrow \emptyset$, $\hat{\mathcal{I}} \leftarrow \emptyset$, $\hat{\mathcal{O}} \leftarrow \emptyset$, $\hat{\mathcal{P}}_{O} \leftarrow \emptyset$, \text{Iterations} $\leftarrow 0$\; \WHILE{\text{Iterations} $< R$} \STATE Randomly select $n$ points as a sample set $\mathcal{S} \subset \widebar{\chi}$ \STATE Estimate $(\hat{s_0}, \hat{R_0}, \hat{t_0})$ on sample set $\mathcal{S}$ by minimizing $\sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} e(\widebar{\chi}_i, \hat{s}_0 ,\hat{R}_0,\hat{t}_0, V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}}, n^*)_i)$ (\cref{Eq:procurstes_align}) \FOR{each point $\widebar{\chi}_i$} \IF{$e(\widebar{\chi}_i, \hat{s_0},\hat{R_0},\hat{t_0}, V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}}, n^*)_i) < \epsilon$} \STATE Add $\widebar{\chi}_i$ to inlier set $\hat{\mathcal{I}}$ \ELSE \STATE Add $\widebar{\chi}_i$ to outlier set $\hat{\mathcal{O}}$ and add $V(\widebar{\mathbf{X}}, n^*)_i$ to outlier SfM set $\hat{\mathcal{P}}_{O}$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \IF{$|\mathcal{I}| > \text{num\_inliers}$} \STATE Update: $\text{num\_inliers} \leftarrow |\mathcal{\hat{I}}|$, $({s_0}, {R_0}, {t_0}) \leftarrow (\hat{s_0}, \hat{R_0}, \hat{t_0})$, $\mathcal{I} \leftarrow \mathcal{\hat{I}}$, $\mathcal{O} \leftarrow \mathcal{\hat{O}}$, $\mathcal{P}_{O} \leftarrow \hat{\mathcal{P}}_{O}$ \ENDIF \STATE \text{Iterations} $\leftarrow$ \text{Iterations} $+ 1$\; \ENDWHILE \STATE return $({s_0}, {R_0}, {t_0})$, $\mathcal{I}$, $\mathcal{O}$, $\mathcal{P}_{O}$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algo}
elements.sent-2411-072: After this, we extract an inlier set $\mathcal{I}$, an outlier set $\mathcal{O}$ and a corresponding outlier SfM set $\mathcal{P _{O}$ from RANSAC with an empirical threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-073: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline , the global transformation recovered from Eq.~\ref{Eq:procurstes_align often leaves large amount of outliers btween $\widebar{\mathbf{X }$ and $\chi$, particularly for objects that are far apart from the foreground.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: We observe that depth generated from DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r or MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding has a strong bias towards smoothness between objects, despite local coherence.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Therefore, we only apply the global $s_0, R_0, t_0$ on inliers $\mathcal{I}$, and handle $\mathcal{O}$ through a secondary Semantic Outlier Alignment step.
elements.sent-2411-076: The key challenge in aligning outliers from $\chi$ to $\widebar{\mathbf{X }$ is that, $\mathcal{P _{O}$ only provides sparse correspondences to $\chi$, which has a 3D point for every pixel.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: As such, some heuristics need to be developed to group regions of $\chi$ to move based on $\mathcal{P _{O}$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Based on the observation that geometric inconsistencies between $\chi$ and $\widebar{\mathbf{X }$ tend to occur between objects and not within objects, we introduce an Interactive Segmentation Model (ISM) to group $\mathcal{P _{O}$ into a series of masks in an iterative manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: First, we randomly select a point from the outlier SfM set, $\widebar{\mathbf{X _k \in \mathcal{P _{O}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Similar to \cref{Eq:project_2D , we can get the projected 2D point $x_k$ to prompt ISM, producing a binary mask $m_k \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We further define a threshold $T$, which describes the desired correspondences to estimate a local alignment.
elements.sent-2411-082: If the number of outliers within $m_k$ is greater than $T$, \ie $| m_k \bigcap \mathcal{P _O| > T$, then we keep $m_k$ as it is and exclude within-mask outliers from being selected in the next iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Otherwise, we use all the outliers within the produced mask to prompt ISM again to update $m_k$ until the above criterion is met or no further correspondences can be included, in which case we discard the local mask and the $x$ within.
elements.sent-2411-084: We repeat the above process until no point is left, eventually obtaining a set of semantically coherent masks around the outliers, $\{ {m _k \}_{k=1 ^{M}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This iterative process can be denoted as \begin{equation}\label{Eq:SAM} \begin{gathered} Init: {m}_k = ISM \left( \{ x_k \in \mathcal{P}_O \setminus \bigcup_{j=1}^{k-1} m_j \} \right) Then, \ \ m_k = \begin{cases} m_k, if\ | m_k \bigcap \mathcal{P}_O | > T ISM(m_k \bigcap \mathcal{P}_O), \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{gathered}\end{equation} Based on the outlier masks $\{ {m _k \}_{k=1 ^{M}$, we extract the within-mask outlier set $\mathcal{O _k = m_k \bigcap \mathcal{O}$ where their corresponding SfM points $\widebar{\mathbf{X _i \in m_k \bigcap \mathcal{P _O$ .}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:SAM} \begin{gathered} Init: {m}_k = ISM \left( \{ x_k \in P_O \setminus \bigcup_{j=1}^{k-1} m_j \} \right) Then, \ \ m_k =  m_k, if\ | m_k \bigcap P_O | > T ISM(m_k \bigcap P_O), otherwise  \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: We then estimate a local rigid transformation within each mask as follows: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:local_procurstes_align} \begin{gathered} s_k, R_k, t_k = \underset{s, R, t}{\arg \min } \sum_{i \in \mathcal{O}_k} \left\|s R \chi_i +t - \widebar{\mathbf{X}}_i \right\|^2. \end{gathered}\end{equation} Finally, we apply all $\{s_k, R_k, t_k \}$ to transform $\chi$ to the coordinate space of $\widebar{\mathbf{X }$.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:local_procurstes_align} \begin{gathered} s_k, R_k, t_k = \underset{s, R, t}{\arg \min } \sum_{i \in O_k} \left\|s R \chi_i +t - \widebar{X}_i \right\|^2. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: To disambiguate inlier and outlier regions, we construct a $m_0 = \neg \left( \bigcup_{k=1 ^{M {m _k \right)$, to indicate inlier regions, where $\neg$ is the logical NOT operator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The global transformation to all $\chi$ within $m_0$ and the individual local transformation to $\chi$ within each $m_k, k\neq 0$ are applied accordingly, resulting in the final SfM-aligned point cloud, $\chi^{*}$. \begin{equation}\label{Eq:final_pcd} \begin{gathered} \chi^{*} = \bigcup_{k=0}^{M} \{ s_k R_k \chi_i + t_k | \chi_i \in m_k \} \end{gathered}\end{equation} To obtain the final dense and pose-wise accurate point cloud $\mathcal{X}$ as prior, we concatenate the final SfM-aligned point cloud with that of SfM, \ie \begin{equation}\label{Eq:final_pcd} \begin{gathered} \mathcal{X} = \chi^{*} \bigcup \widebar{\mathbf{X}} \end{gathered}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: The SPARS3R constructed $\mathcal{X}$ can be directly used as the initialization for any Gaussian Splatting-based optimization method.
elements.sent-2411-090: Here we use Splatfacto, developed under the NeRFStudio framework~\cite{tancik2023nerfstudio ; the Gaussian optimization loss is: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:training_loss} \begin{gathered} \mathcal{L} = \lambda_1 \| \tilde{C} - C \|_1 + \lambda_2 D-SSIM(\tilde{C},C), \end{gathered}\end{equation} where $\tilde{C}$ and $C$ denote our rendered and the groundtruth RGB image, $D-SSIM$ denotes the structural similarity loss.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Experiments are performed on three popular benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-092: Tanks and Temples~\cite{knapitsch2017tanks contains 8 scenes captured in video format.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Following InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat , We uniformly sample 24 images with alternating indices for train and test split.}
elements.sent-2411-094: MVimgNet~\cite{yu2023mvimgnet is an object-centric dataset that consists of 7 scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-095: These scenes are captured from a consistent height covering 180° field of view.
elements.sent-2411-096: We apply the same sampling and train-test split procedure as for Tanks and Template.
elements.sent-2411-097: Mip-NeRF 360~\cite{barron2022mip360 comprises of 9 scenes with 360° views and greater pose variation between the scenes, including diverse heights and distances.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The increased variability, combined with sparsity, adds complexity to accurate pose estimation and scene reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-099: For this dataset, we follow the test set outlined in MipNeRF360~\cite{barron2022mip360 and uniformly sample 12 images from the original training set to construct a sparse-view set.}
elements.sent-2411-100: All sparse-view input images go through a COLMAP \cite{schoenberger2016colmap for camera calibration.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, we use feature matching results from MASt3R~\cite{leroy2024grounding for SfM triangulation.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Since sparse-view registration can be unstable due to limited pairs, we perform multiple SfMs and pick the outcome that maximizes successful triangulation per image.
elements.sent-2411-103: Accurately and holistically evaluating sparse NVS should involve many practical considerations.
elements.sent-2411-104: Various NeRF-based approaches~\cite{yu2021pixelnerf, jain2021putting, niemeyer2022regnerf, wang2023sparsenerf, yang2023freenerf, kim2022infonerf, deng2022depth have assumed perfect camera poses, i.e. calibration results from dense views, for sparse-view scenarios, which is unrealistic.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Gaussian-Splatting-based methods rely heavily on SfM point cloud, further necessitating the separation of train and test views from the registration stage.
elements.sent-2411-106: Previous approaches~\cite{zhu2025fsgs have tried to perform re-triangulation based on known train poses, but do not account for pose inaccuracy in sparse-view registration.}
elements.sent-2411-107: InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat applies Procrustes Alignment~\cite{gower1975generalized between the camera poses from train-images-only calibration and the ground-truth poses to initialize test camera poses.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: While such alignment process removes the involvement of practically inaccessible information, camera alignment error leads to significant disruption in common pixel-based rendering metrics, e.g. PSNR and SSIM~\cite{wang2004image .}

elements.sent-2411-109: We improve upon the previous camera alignment process in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-110: Firstly, instead of relying on camera position only, we sample three additional points $P_\text{rotation \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times3}$ per camera along the unit direction vectors in rotation matrix $R_\text{camera \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times3}$ at camera position $T_\text{camera \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times3}$. \begin{equation}P_\text{rotation} = \left(s_{\text{cloud}}R_\text{camera}+T_\text{camera}\right)^T,\end{equation} where the scale of the camera cloud, denoted by $s_{\text{cloud = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+\sigma_z^2}$, where $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\sigma_z$ are the standard deviations of all camera positions along $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: These four points per camera allow camera alignment to align camera rotations and translations.
elements.sent-2411-112: Secondly, we add RANSAC~\cite{fischler1981random in the alignment process, minimizing the influence of outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-113: We measure the alignment rotation error $E_R$ by the angular difference between two rotations represented by quaternions.

elements.sent-2411-114: \begin{equation}E_R=2 \cdot \arccos \left(\frac{\text{abs}(q_1 \cdot q_2)}{\left\|q_1\right\| \cdot\left\|q_2\right\|}\right),\end{equation} and the translation error $E_T$ is computed by the Euclidean norm $\left\| \cdot \right\|$ between translation vectors.
elements.sent-2411-115: As shown in \cref{tab:align_error , these two methods improve camera alignment accuracy in both rotation and translation.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Beyond accurate camera pose alignment, test pose optimization can also be applied between rendered and ground-truth images to minimize the pose error; however, such process is time-consuming and can get stuck if the initial displacement is too large, as is sometimes the case in InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat .}
elements.sent-2411-117: We also propose to use DreamSim~\cite{fu2023dreamsim (DSIM) as an additional metric to assess render quality.}
elements.sent-2411-118: DSIM seeks to represent human perceptual similarity by finetuning a combination of embeddings from visual foundation models based on human evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-119: As demonstrated in Fig. \ref{fig:dsim_exp , given several ground-truth images with small pose differences, PSNR and SSIM metric drop significantly with slight pose perturbation and cannot express the quality of the render.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Despite also measuring perceptual similarity, LPIPS is not as pose-shift invariant as DSIM, likely due to the patch-based convolutional design.
elements.sent-2411-121: In comparison, DSIM is the most pose-shift invariant metric out of the four.

elements.sent-2411-127: SfM-initialized 3DGS: Optimizing 3DGS on a sparse SfM point cloud and SfM-estimated camera poses.
elements.sent-2411-128: MASt3R-initialized 3DGS: Optimizing 3DGS on a MASt3R-produced dense point cloud and camera poses.
elements.sent-2411-129: Global Fusion Alignment: Aligning the MASt3R-produced dense point cloud onto SfM point cloud with a global transform.
elements.sent-2411-131: Semantic Outlier Alignment (SPARS3R): The full SPARS3R pipeline that aligns outliers through their 2D semantic masks.
elements.sent-2411-132: As shown in \cref{tab:ablation , given only 12 images, sparse NVS is challenging both in registration and reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Specifically, we observe that the 3DGS based on SfM initialization performs better than MASt3R initialization.
elements.sent-2411-134: Clearly, render quality is affected if poses are inaccurate, despite locally coherent dense point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-135: The introduction of Global Fusion Alignment improves upon the naive baselines from either initializations by combining accurate pose from SfM and dense point cloud from MASt3R.
elements.sent-2411-136: For scenes that do not have significant depth discrepancies, Global Fusion Alignment works well.
elements.sent-2411-137: In scenarios such as the Bonsai scene in Fig.~\ref{tab:four_images , MASt3R's relative depth between the table and the wall is inaccurate; therefore, Global Fusion Alignment alone leads to a broken geometry of the background.}
elements.sent-2411-138: In comparison, our Semantic Outlier Alignment provide piece-wise alignments on the background to address this relative discrepancy.
elements.sent-2411-139: Quantitatively, we observe a 1.4 dB improvement on the Bonsai scene.

elements.sent-2411-149: Among methods that are designed for sparse NVS, DRGS~\cite{chung2024drgs ,SparseGS~\cite{xiong2023sparsegs , and FSGS~\cite{zhu2025fsgs leverage monocular depth prior to gain more geometric information of the scene.}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: DRGS applies an additional smoothness constraint, which leads to high PSNR values but worse LPIPS and DSIM performances.
elements.sent-2411-151: SparseGS additionally applies a Score Distillation Sampling Loss, which generates novel views and constrained them through a prior generative model.
elements.sent-2411-152: Its results are sharper than DRGS, but more noisy, leading to worse PSNR.
elements.sent-2411-153: FSGS seeks to address the sparse point cloud issue in sparse-view NVS by modifying the densification algorithm in 3DGS.
elements.sent-2411-154: More Gaussians are encouraged between the space of two existing Gaussians, allowing representative areas to densify quicker.
elements.sent-2411-155: FSGS has overall better metrics than SparseGS and DRGS; however, it still encounters issues in regions where few initializations are available, e.g. the background of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-156: As shown in Fig.~\ref{tab:intro and in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser , this leads to various blurry regions.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: InstantSplat~\cite{fan2024instantsplat is a concurrent work that uses prior depth estimation from DUSt3R~\cite{wang2024dust3r and the dense point cloud to help with sparse-view NVS.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: While the initialized point cloud is much denser than FSGS, InstantSplat also inherits the suboptimal poses produced by DUSt3R.
elements.sent-2411-159: The incorrect geometry limits the effectiveness of densification in 3DGS and can lead to generation of floaters; to ameliorate this problem, InstantSplat disables Gaussian densification and optimizes the scene with few steps.
elements.sent-2411-160: As shown in \cref{tab:pose and \cref{tab:my_label , on the MipNeRF360 and MVimgNet dataset, where pose accuracy from DUSt3R is limited, InstantSplat performs on-par with FSGS despite using a denser prior point cloud.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: As shown in Fig.~\ref{tab:intro , we observe that the Gaussians tend to be noisy on the surface.}
elements.sent-2411-162: In the Stump and Bicycle scenes, InstantSplat's results cannot be rigidly aligned well with the groundtruth test poses due to the large offset to begin with.
elements.sent-2411-163: Our SPARS3R method improves upon DUSt3R and MASt3R's prior point cloud by adjusting it based on conventional SfM calibration approaches.
elements.sent-2411-164: Since SfM only triangulates based on confident keypoints, its pose and depth estimation performance, particularly under sparse-view scenario, is more reliable.
elements.sent-2411-165: As shown in \cref{tab:my_label , SPARS3R consistently improves upon previous best approach by an average of 2.7 dB.}
elements.sent-2411-166: SPARS3R is stable in its optimization process and does not generate additional floaters with more iterations.
elements.sent-2411-167: As seen in Fig.~\ref{tab:four_images , SPARS3R produces rendering with smooth surfaces in the foreground, and sharp details in the background.}
elements.sent-2411-168: For more visualization and flythrough of the scene, please refer to the Supplemental Material section.
elements.sent-2411-169: While SPARS3R significantly improves upon previous SoTA, there are also several limitations worth noting.
elements.sent-2411-170: Firstly, SPARS3R relies on a well-performing semantic segmentation model to group outliers; as such, if the segmentation model is very sensitive, i.e. separating a connected surface into multiple disjointed parts, some of these parts may lack sufficient SfM support for alignment.
elements.sent-2411-171: If the segmentation model produces masks that group multiple areas with disparate depth together, the local alignments are also less effective similar to the global alignment.
elements.sent-2411-172: Secondly, non-rigid transform between two point clouds are worth further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-173: While SPARS3R's piece-wise rigid transform based on semantic relationship is fast and addresses the inaccurate inter-object depth estimation, a non-rigid formulation may be more generalizable.
elements.sent-2411-174: We present SPARS3R, a scene reconstruction and NVS method that can achieve high-quality rendering with sparse input images.
elements.sent-2411-175: We demonstrate that existing methods lead to blurry rendering due to sparse point cloud initialization.
elements.sent-2411-176: Recent progress in generating a dense point cloud from prior depth estimation model, while promising, can lead to noisy pose estimation.
elements.sent-2411-177: To address these issues, SPARS3R combines the advantages of both by proposing a two-step alignment approach.
elements.sent-2411-178: The first step computes a global transformation matrix between the dense point cloud from depth prior and a reference SfM point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-179: The second step takes the semantic regions of the outlier points from the first step and performs successive local alignments.
elements.sent-2411-180: This approach successfully addresses the depth estimation errors within the prior dense point cloud and demonstrates that the updated point cloud leads to significantly better performances in sparse-view NVS.
elements.sent-2411-181: We also introduce several improvements in the evaluation process to better represent the practical limitations in sparse-view registration and reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-182: In the future, we hope to further improve the smoothness of our alignment process, e.g. by exploring potential non-rigid transformation approaches.

elements.sent-2411-186: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-188: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-189: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-190: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-191: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-192: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-193: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-194: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-195: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-196: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-197: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-198: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-199: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-200: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-201: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-202: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-204: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-207: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-208: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-209: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-210: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-211: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-212: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-213: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-011: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-215: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-218: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-219: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-220: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-221: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-222: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
